Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Run forward, don't look back: Reflections on the moment Manus's trade was rescinded
data.description: On April 27th, the deal between Manus and Meta, which had been rumored for months, finally received its final result. It wasn't "continued review." It wasn't "supplementary materials." It was a ban on investment and a demand to cancel the deal. These words carry weight. Because they express not just a regular business opinion, but an attitude. Meta losing a company isn't the end of the world. Manus losing $2 billion isn't the first time the AI ​​industry has seen a bubble burst. In the startup world, stories of failed fundraising, failed mergers, and zero valuations are commonplace. But this time it's different. In the past, many entrepreneurs were used to viewing their companies as purely commercial entities. A good product, user growth, rising valuation, and capital exit—this was a very natural path. But AI is not the business of the previous generation of the internet. AI is not a new category of apps, nor is it a smarter office tool. AI is becoming the deciding factor in the next round of competition. Whoever masters model capabilities controls the next generation of software entry points. Whoever masters intelligent agent products may control the next generation of workflows. Whoever controls AI infrastructure and the application ecosystem will have an extra card to play in the future division of labor in the industry. It's not that a company stumbled. It's that an old world model has failed. PART.01: The Old World Model Has Failed. For the past decade or so, Chinese entrepreneurs have had a very mature, default script in mind. People are in China, the market is in China, engineers are in China, and products are developed in China. But funding can be in US dollars, legal entities can be based in the Cayman Islands, IPOs can be in the US, and offices can be moved to Hong Kong, Singapore, or Silicon Valley when necessary. This system has been operating for many years. The underlying premise is: China needs growth, the US needs assets, capital needs exits, and entrepreneurs need a story. In that era, everyone cooperated in a gray area. Success meant growing the company, allowing capital to exit, and enabling the founders to cash out. The core conflict wasn't "who owns the technology," but rather "how to grow the company, how to exit capital, and how to continue growth." As long as this overarching logic held true, much ambiguity could be tolerated. You could have a Chinese business, dollar capital, an offshore structure, or a US listing. Everyone knew there were many gray areas, but that era was willing to leave them an outlet. But AI is different. AI isn't group buying, food delivery, e-commerce, or even just an upgrade to a short video recommendation algorithm. AI has been placed within the framework of competition today. Model capabilities, engineering talent, training data, inference systems, intelligent agent products, and commercialization entry points—any one of these can be seen as a strategic asset. If you still use the worldview of 2010s internet companies to deal with AI companies in 2026, you'll run into problems. Many people don't have a problem with their judgment, but rather that their worldview hasn't been updated. They still think this is a game of capital, but the tables have changed. Previously, you mainly dealt with investors, users, exchanges, and M&A lawyers. Now you also have to deal with security reviews, export controls, technological boundaries, and competition. This isn't just happening in China. The US is changing too. In the past, global capital believed in efficiency. Capital flowed to where there was cheaper talent; companies went where there were larger markets; projects went public where valuations were higher. The underlying logic of globalization was the efficiency of resource allocation. But today, the underlying logic of globalization is becoming boundaries. Technology has boundaries. Data has boundaries. Computing power has boundaries. Capital has boundaries. Talent mobility is also beginning to have boundaries. This is what many entrepreneurs find hardest to accept. It's not that they've stopped working hard, or that their products have no future; it's that the mental map in their heads is outdated. What entrepreneurs fear most isn't hard work. Entrepreneurs all know hard work. What entrepreneurs fear most is running desperately forward only to find halfway there that the map has changed. PART.02 Position, Position, and Position Again In the business world, many people like to talk about ability. Product ability, fundraising ability, growth ability, organizational ability, technical ability, and narrative ability. These are all important, of course. But in this era of great change, the most important thing is often not ability, but position. Where do you stand? To whom do you prove your value? From whose environment did you grow? To whom do you entrust your technological assets at crucial moments? These questions seem abstract in everyday life. Entrepreneurs don't like to write about them in their fundraising presentations, and investors don't necessarily like to ask them. Everyone is more concerned with ARR, DAU, retention, valuation, and who will invest in the next round. That's how the business world is; with too many metrics, it's easy to pretend you're very clear-headed. But once the documents are finalized, these issues become anything but abstract. Manus's problem isn't its desire to internationalize. Chinese companies can certainly internationalize. Nor is it its desire to earn US dollars. A startup wanting to exit isn't inherently wrong. The real problem is that it seems to have misunderstood its place. If you decide from day one to be a completely American company, you should register, raise funds, hire, conduct R&D, serve customers, and accept local regulations in the US from day one. If you decide from day one to be a completely Singaporean company, you should build your team, conduct business, establish compliance, and accept local rules in Singapore from day one. These are all choices. But if you grew up in the Chinese tech ecosystem, benefited from the Chinese engineer dividend, and enjoyed the attention garnered by the Chinese AI startup narrative, and then, during a period of skyrocketing valuations, structurally packaged yourself as a "non-Chinese asset" and ultimately sold to an American giant, problems will arise. Because in this era, identity isn't something you declare yourself. Identity is determined by your history. Where you accumulate technology, where you recruit core talent, where you gain initial attention, where you validate your product, and where you build team capabilities—all these things become part of your identity. Entrepreneurs can change their registered location. They can change their offices. You can change the financing entity. You can change the PR approach. But it's difficult to change your own growth history. This is the coldest point about the Manus incident. It doesn't just ask where you are now. It also asks where you came from. PART.03 Be Valuable In China, many tech entrepreneurs are unwilling to talk about risk, which is understandable. Entrepreneurs like to talk about products, users, cash flow, and the next round of financing. Everything else sounds too distant, too heavy, and too easy to make people uncomfortable. But not talking about it doesn't mean it doesn't exist. When grand narratives haven't yet impacted your fundraising, exits, compliance, mergers and acquisitions, and team turnover, you can certainly pretend you're just an ordinary entrepreneur. But once AI is introduced into the competition, even if you don't talk about it, it will talk about you. A tech company's value within the system can be roughly categorized into three states. The first is positive value. You can fill technological gaps, enhance industrial capabilities, and give China more leverage in a key area. You don't necessarily need to shout slogans, but your existence objectively strengthens the system. The second is zero value. You're just an ordinary commercial company, making your own products and earning your own money. Not particularly important, nor particularly dangerous. The system may not care about you, and you may not have any impact on the overall situation. The third type is negative value. You originally grew up within the Chinese technology system, but might end up handing over your key teams, technological assets, product experience, and strategic narrative to an American giant. At this point, it's not a question of "whether you've made a contribution," but rather "whether you'll create a demonstration effect." If you become negative value, you're easily made a typical example. This sounds harsh, but it's a sober assessment. It's not because a particular company is especially important, but because it represents a path. What the system needs to eliminate is often not a single company, but the path itself. If Manus's path succeeds, what will happen next? A group of Chinese AI entrepreneurs will see this path. First, establish technology and brand awareness in China. Then move to Singapore. Then sell to a major American company. Then explain everything as "global entrepreneurship." If this path proves successful, it will set a very bad example for the Chinese AI ecosystem. Because it tells newcomers: You can first reap the benefits of China's technology ecosystem, grow big, and then cut yourself out, ultimately being acquired and integrated by American tech giants. From an entrepreneur's perspective, this is certainly tempting. But from a competitive perspective, it's a different story. So the Manus deal being halted isn't about Manus itself. The key point is telling newcomers: This path is not viable. PART.04 You can't have it all, and you can't have it all, and you can't have it all. Therefore, this doesn't mean all entrepreneurs must stay, nor does it mean all Chinese-background companies can't go global. On the contrary. AI startups should be globalized. Good products should serve global users. Chinese entrepreneurs shouldn't confine themselves to a single market. Tools, agents, content production, enterprise automation, and developer services naturally have global markets. But going global and cutting ties are not the same thing. Globalization and arbitrage are also not the same thing. You can choose, but don't fantasize about not paying the price. If you don't want to be part of China's technology system, you should have left from day one. Go to the US, Singapore, Europe—it's all fine. From day one, you're there getting funding, hiring, building a company, developing products, and accepting local regulations. This is a clear choice. You can also choose to stay. Acknowledge your place within China's technology system, serve Chinese industries, participate in China's AI ecosystem, accept the rules, and clearly understand the relationship between your capital, market, technology, and system. This is also a clear choice. The truly dangerous path is the middle one. It requires "China speed" at the outset, a dollar valuation during fundraising, a global halo during dissemination, and a takeover bid from a major American company upon exit. The system arrives, and then it claims to be just an ordinary commercial company. This isn't globalization. This is failing to make a choice. Many people used to call this state "clever." Because in a period of peace, ambiguity is a space. You can maneuver between different rules, switch between different markets, and seek maximum benefit within different narratives. But in a period of conflict, ambiguity is risk. When the US-China technological competition enters the AI ​​realm, many things that could previously be handled with ambiguity will be redefined. Previously, you could say that capital has no borders. Now people ask, who has benefited from capital? Previously, you could say that technology has no borders. Now people ask, where does technology ultimately flow? Previously, you could say that startups are merely business entities. Now people ask, on which side will this company stand in the next round of industrial competition? This isn't just a question China is asking. The US is asking it too. So it's not just China that's become stricter; the whole world has changed. Previously, global capital believed in efficiency; now, national competition believes in boundaries. If entrepreneurs fail to recognize this change and continue to apply the overseas expansion models of previous-generation internet companies to AI companies, that's not courage, it's misjudgment. PART.05 Run Forward, Don't Look Back So the reminder for all AI entrepreneurs is actually very simple: Run forward, don't look back. This isn't motivational rhetoric. It's not telling you to blindly rush in. It means: if you choose a path, you must accept the costs of that path. If you want to build an American company, then start as an American company from day one. If you want to build a Singaporean company, then start as a Singaporean company from day one. If you want to run a Chinese company, acknowledge that you're within the Chinese system and clearly understand your relationships with technology, capital, markets, and regulations. Every position has its advantages. Every position also has its costs. The most common mistake entrepreneurs make is only wanting the benefits of each position without considering the costs. If you choose China, don't fantasize about designing your business entirely according to the exit logic of the previous generation of dollar-denominated internet companies. If you choose the US, don't fantasize about perpetually leveraging the Chinese technology ecosystem at low cost. If you choose Singapore, don't assume it's just a neutral shell that can erase all your historical origins. The most dangerous people are those who never want to make any choices. This is an era that demands a deeper understanding of one's place. You need to know who you are. You need to know where you come from. You need to know to whom you need to prove your value. You also need to know which side you're on when the tides of history begin to turn. This isn't a moral performance. This is strategic judgment. Many entrepreneurs like to say they only care about the product, the users, and cash flow. That's certainly true. But if a changing era can determine your fundraising, exit strategy, mergers and acquisitions, compliance, and company identity, then recognizing the era itself is also a form of entrepreneurial ability. This might even be the most important entrepreneurial skill. Because if the product is wrong, it can be corrected. If the fundraising timing is wrong, it can be corrected. But if the positioning is wrong, the cost will be extremely high. In the face of a changing era, intelligence is no longer the most important thing. Position is. Run forward, don't look back. Not because what's ahead is necessarily safe. But because the path back is no longer there.

data.title: Is the tokenization of gold under the shadow of a volatile market just a gimmick or the next dark horse?
data.description:
1. Background

In recent years, significant price fluctuations have propelled gold, a traditional asset, back to the forefront of the market: in 2025, the annual increase in gold prices exceeded 50%, and in January of this year, it even broke through the $5,000/ounce mark, once approaching $5,600/ounce. However, since March, with the continued escalation of geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East and the impact of energy prices, gold prices have plummeted, directly erasing all of last year's gains. This dramatic market shift has made the term "gold monkey market" a viral sensation on social media. Parallel to this is the heated narrative of "gold tokenization"—on the other side of the financial world, people are actively exploring more possibilities for gold on the blockchain.

On March 19th, the World Gold Council (RWA) announced the construction of a new shared infrastructure for the digital gold market and proposed the concept of "Gold as a Service" (GAS). This involves developing a digital issuance and management system that connects physical gold custody with gold-backed products, aiming to provide a standardized system for the issuance, trading, and management of various digital gold assets. Meanwhile, tokenized gold assets such as PAXG and XAUt have evolved from concepts into a large-scale market, with major institutions accelerating their strategic deployments in related fields. Gold tokenization provides a new way to express the value of traditional gold. Compared to physical gold and other gold products, tokenized gold has a lower investment threshold, supports 24/7 trading, and ensures investor rights through standardized custody, third-party auditing, and redemption mechanisms, providing investors with a safe and convenient alternative investment channel. Therefore, many believe that gold tokenization will be the next "dark horse" in the RWA sector. Is gold tokenization a "dark horse" or just a "gimmick"? This question deserves deep consideration. To find the answer, we cannot rely solely on narrative sentiment; we must return to three key questions: Gold already has many product forms. How does tokenization differ from physical gold and gold ETFs, and does it offer genuine functional growth? Has the market and regulatory system for tokenized gold formed a transparent market sufficient to support investor trust? What significant risks will project teams and ordinary investors planning to invest in tokenized gold face? 2. The Current Market Status of Tokenized Gold 2.1 Core Concepts Tokenized gold refers to the conversion of ownership, beneficial rights, or other economic interests of physical gold into on-chain digital tokens through blockchain and smart contract technology. Typically, each token is pegged to a certain weight of physical gold, which is held in a vault by a gold custodian. Since the gold tokens are on-chain, users can send them to other people's wallet addresses or conduct on-chain transactions just like using other cryptocurrencies. The operation of tokenized gold has several key components: (1) Custody: The project team purchases physical gold and stores it in a secure and insured vault to ensure that the digital tokens have the value backing of real-world assets; (2) Minting: The project team uses smart contracts on the blockchain to mint digital tokens, and the number of minted tokens matches the amount of gold in reserve; (3) Redemption: Some gold tokenization projects support physical redemption. When users exchange tokens for physical gold, the system will destroy the corresponding number of tokens to keep the number of tokens consistent with the gold reserves; (4) Reconciliation, auditing and verification: In order to maintain trust in the decentralized system, the project team needs to hire an independent third-party auditing institution to conduct regular inventory and verification. Some projects also use oracle networks to provide proof of reserve. Taking Tether Gold (XAUt) as an example, each XAUt token represents ownership of one ounce (31.1035 grams) of gold (typically 99.9% pure) held in a Swiss vault and meeting the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) delivery standards. The operating entity, TG Commodities, purchases physical gold from the Swiss gold market and stores it in vaults. Investors can query the unique serial number, weight, and purity of a specific gold bar through a blockchain address. There are two paths to redeem funds: one is to redeem the entire physical gold bar, which requires users to hold at least a certain amount of XAUt to initiate an application; the other is to exchange XAUt for US dollars or Tether (USDT) through compliant channels such as Bitfinex.

2.2 Market Development Status

Currently, tokenized gold is dominated by Tether Gold (XAUt) and PAX Gold (PAXG), which together hold the vast majority of the market share. At the same time, there are also non-mainstream gold token products such as Kinesis Gold (KAU), Pleasing Gold (PGOLD), and Matrixdock Gold (XAUm), which have established their own advantages through differentiated competition, but their scale is relatively small. In addition, the World Gold Council plans to launch a token called "Pooled Gold Interests" (PGI) in 2026, adopting a trust rights model different from the physical collateral structure of mainstream tokens such as XAUt and PAXG, further promoting market diversification under the duopoly competition pattern.

In terms of market size, according to relevant statistics (data source: Rwa.xyz), as of April 17, 2026, the total market capitalization of tokenized gold reached $5.27 billion. A research report (Cex.io) shows that the total trading volume of tokenized gold in 2025 reached $178 billion, surging to over $126 billion in the fourth quarter, far exceeding any previous period and even slightly surpassing the combined volume of the five largest gold ETFs. The investor base is also gradually expanding, with the number of tokenized gold holders increasing by more than 115,000 last year, 14 times faster than the growth rate in 2024.

Figure 1 Estimated total market value of tokenized gold

3. What are the differences between tokenized gold: A comparison of gold investment tools

As a traditional safe-haven asset, gold plays an important role in investment portfolios. Besides tokenized gold, the main ways investors acquire gold exposure include physical gold, gold ETFs, and bank-accumulated gold. Physical gold exists in the form of gold bars, coins, or jewelry, and is the most direct and traditional investment method. Investors directly purchase and hold physical gold, with clear ownership and a visible asset form. Gold ETFs, short for Gold Exchange Traded Funds, are listed and traded on stock exchanges. Investors buy and sell ETF shares through securities accounts, with equity shares backed by physical gold, indirectly obtaining returns from gold price fluctuations. Accumulated gold is a type of gold investment product offered by commercial banks. Investors can accumulate gold shares by amount or weight through regular investment, piecemeal purchases, etc., and manage them through accounts. Different gold investment tools differ in ownership structure, trading methods, and usage scenarios, thus forming their own advantages and disadvantages.

To address the inconvenience of storing physical gold and the high processing costs, products like gold ETFs offer investors more efficient trading tools, but they still suffer from pain points such as multiple intermediaries in centralized settlement, transaction delays, and limited trading hours. Tokenized gold offers an innovative solution to these problems: First, it links physical gold to digital tokens, achieving gold investment functions similar to traditional products like gold ETFs, and further lowering the investment threshold through the division of property rights; second, it eliminates trading time limitations, supporting instant, high-frequency trading scenarios, and generally has lower management fees than traditional products. Theoretically, investors can obtain better liquidity with lower transaction costs; third, it expands the sources of income for gold investment tools. Tokenized gold is expected to transcend the traditional function of gold as a diversified investment and store of value, transforming it into deployable capital and giving rise to new application scenarios such as gold pledging and lending. Investors can not only profit from the price difference between buying and selling gold but also potentially enjoy the returns brought by DeFi. Different investment instruments arise from varying user needs, making it impossible to definitively say which form can completely replace others; however, there is no doubt that tokenization has created new possibilities for the acquisition and trading of gold. During the closure of traditional products such as gold futures and ETFs, tokenized gold assumes a crucial price discovery function. As a long-standing hard currency, gold has become more accessible and easier to trade through the innovative development of tokenization, possessing unique value in both enriching investor choices and promoting the integration of gold assets into the modern financial system.

Table 1 Comparison of different gold investment methods

2 Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Gold Investment Instruments

4. Regulatory Landscape and Compliance Challenges of Tokenized Gold

4.1 Regulatory Models in Major Jurisdictions

(3) Hong Kong, China: If a tokenized gold project is defined as a "security token," it falls under the regulatory scope of the Securities and Futures Ordinance (SFO). Issuers must comply with the requirements of the prospectus or private placement exemption and conduct customer due diligence and source of funds review in accordance with the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance (AMLO). In addition, providing related issuance and trading services requires obtaining a VASP compliance license based on the Virtual Asset Service Provider Licensing System.

4.2 Potential Compliance Challenges

The wheels of technological innovation are rolling forward, and complex compliance issues are gradually becoming more prominent. First, the underlying assets provide the most basic credit for gold tokens. Whether the project structure and audit mechanism can effectively control the risks of the underlying assets is the primary issue that the project team needs to address. This requires project teams to ensure clear ownership of physical gold, free from any defects in title, and to pay attention to whether the gold is subject to any encumbrances such as mortgages or pledges when purchasing it. The source and quality of the gold must be rigorously verified and subject to regular third-party audits. Furthermore, full, truthful, and timely information disclosure is essential, including details of the underlying assets, risk factors, and profit mechanisms, to avoid regulatory penalties and class-action lawsuits due to false statements or concealment. Secondly, cross-border compliance and continuous operation also pose challenges to the compliance capabilities of project teams. Gold has global circulation attributes, and the issuance location, custody location, and investor location of gold tokens may be in different countries or regions, subject to multiple legal jurisdictions. This requires project teams to systematically review the regulatory requirements and compliance obligations that their business activities may trigger and respond appropriately. Finally, in terms of taxation, the legal attributes and tax characterization of tokenized gold are ambiguous, and the nature of the revenue from gold tokenization projects will significantly affect the applicable tax types and tax burden levels. Meanwhile, from the asset side to the trading side, tokenized gold projects may trigger tax obligations at multiple stages and points, including issuance, profit distribution, and secondary market trading. This requires project teams and investors to conduct reasonable tax planning by keeping abreast of tax policies and seeking professional assistance. 5. Conclusion Gold tokenization is perhaps one of the most eye-catching intersections between traditional finance and Web3 this year. Returning to the question posed at the beginning, gold tokenization is neither a meaningless repackaging of old wine nor a dark horse to bet on unconditionally. On the one hand, tokenized gold increases the accessibility and liquidity of gold, possessing unique value compared to traditional investment categories, and has already formed a market of a certain scale, possessing good development potential driven by global policies and institutional deployments. On the other hand, we must also recognize that the value foundation of tokenized gold is still being strengthened. Factors such as the security of the underlying assets, the quality of third-party audits, information disclosure and redemption mechanisms, and the global regulatory framework will all affect its ability to secure a place in the fierce market competition. In any case, the future of gold is being rewritten on the blockchain, and all of this is happening now.

data.title: MSTR STRC In-Depth Research: The Financing Flywheel Behind the 11.5% Yield
data.description:
Author｜Benji @ IOSG

Core Viewpoint: STRC is a cleverly designed funding tool that transforms fixed-income demand into buying pressure on Bitcoin. In a bull market, it can provide an 11.5% floating return with low price volatility, but its risk structure is essentially equivalent to "selling a put option" on Bitcoin asset coverage, so it cannot replace true fixed-income products when BTC falls.

STRC's real vulnerability is not the BTC price, but mNAV. Once MSTR's mNAV falls below 1.0x for more than 4 consecutive weeks, the flywheel will enter a passive downward spiral within 3 months.

We estimate there's a 70% probability this trigger will occur in the second half of 2026, at which point STRC will present a buy-in entry point of $85-$90. If the trigger doesn't occur, it means Saylor has successfully created a completely new category of native BTC credit instruments. Background: Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) launched STRC (“Stretch”), a perpetual preferred stock with a target par value of $100, maintaining price stability through monthly floating dividends. As of March 31, 2026, STRC had a notional size of $5 billion, with peak daily trading volume exceeding $300 million (data as of March 2026). Since its launch, it has provided Strategy with over $3.5 billion in BTC purchase funds, making it its most important current financing vehicle. As of April 12, 2026, Strategy's balance sheet held 780,897 BTC, with a leverage ratio of 33%, and approximately $21.6 billion in remaining STRC ATM issuance capacity. This instrument belongs to a novel category: it resembles a money market fund (stable price, high yield), but its credit risk stems entirely from a single company's BTC holdings. Before elaborating on the argument, let's clarify "where we might be going wrong." If our analysis is wrong, it will be because: traditional fixed-income investors are genuinely willing to accept reflexive risk for a 700bps spread; STRC has reached $50 billion in size within 3 years, becoming a de facto BTC yield curve; Saylor has successfully securitized BTC into an interest-bearing collateralized asset acceptable to institutional portfolios. This outcome would represent the largest integration of crypto into traditional finance to date—a new asset class of over $50 billion that simply didn't exist before 2025. In this optimistic scenario, the dividend suspension in April 2026 is not a warning sign, but rather a characteristic: a mature instrument begins to stabilize its yields after early price discovery, similar to the gradual downward repricing of early high-yield bond ETFs as institutions adopt them.

Argument Breakdown

STRC's core innovation: It transforms yield-seeking funds into buying pressure for BTC. When STRC trading around $100, Saylor issues new shares through ATMs (approximately 40% of daily trading volume), uses the proceeds to purchase BTC, and then issues MSTR common stock at a price higher than NAV (mNAV>1x) to complete deleveraging. The end result is: $100M of daily STRC trading volume can leverage approximately $120M of BTC purchases.

Argument Breakdown

STRC's core innovation: It transforms yield-seeking funds into buying pressure for BTC. When STRC trading is around $100, Saylor issues new shares through ATMs (approximately 40% of daily trading volume), uses the proceeds to purchase BTC, and then issues MSTR common stock at a price higher than NAV (mNAV>1x) to complete deleveraging.

Argument Breakdown

STRC's core innovation: It transforms yield-seeking funds into buying pressure for BTC.

However, the vulnerability of this mechanism lies in its underlying cyclical nature: the STRC remains stable at $100 because investors believe it will remain stable; and Saylor maintains this belief by continuously raising dividends. This anchor is not backed by collateral, but by confidence, sustained by a continuous dividend auction without a formal cap. Once this confidence breaks down, the auction will become increasingly expensive.

Evidence and Comparison: STRC vs. Other Bitcoin Exposure Tools

When will STRC collapse: Death Spiral Scenario

The key question is: Will STRC enter a self-reinforcing downward cycle? The answer is yes, but under certain conditions. This mechanism has three interconnected failure paths.

Phase 1: BTC drops and breaks the $100 anchor

When BTC drops sharply (such as a 45% retracement from its all-time high at the end of 2025), Strategy's leverage ratio will mechanically increase.

Based on 780,897 BTC and a 33% leverage ratio (as of April 12, 2026, MSTR 8-K), if BTC drops another 50%, the leverage ratio will be pushed to approximately 66%. At this point, STRC's credit quality deteriorates because its priority claim on the remaining assets becomes thinner. The price falls below $100. This has already happened three times (August 2025: approximately $92, November 2025: intraday low, February 2026: approximately $93), but each time BTC quickly rebounded, pulling the anchor back up. Phase Two: The Dividend Increase Trap According to Strategy's guidance submitted to the SEC: if monthly VWAP is between $95 and $99, the dividend yield increases by 25 bps per month; if it falls below $95, it increases by 50 bps per month. From 9% to 11.5%, the dividend yield has cumulatively increased by 250 bps in approximately 8 months (August 2025 to April 2026), averaging about 31 bps per month—a rate faster than the repricing of any similar company's preferred stock in stable market conditions. April 2026 marks the first pause after seven consecutive increases. Two interpretations: (a) Demand stabilizes – bullish; (b) Strategy has hit the yield ceiling that traditional fixed-income buyers are sensitive to – bearish. This is the single signal most worth tracking in the next 1–2 months. If BTC continues to be sluggish, dividends must continue to rise to attract buyers back to near par value. At a $5B scale, every 100bps increase means approximately $50M of additional cash expenditure per year; if STRC expands to $20B (authorized ATM limits), the cost of every 100bps becomes $200M per year. A bear market lasting more than 6 months at the current upward pace would push STRC's yield to 13-15%. At this level, annual dividend payouts of $20 billion would exceed $2.6-3 billion, consuming a significant portion of Strategy BTC's potential returns and forcing it to choose between "continuing to raise dividends" and "abandoning the stability narrative." There is no formal cap on dividend increases; this "unlimited" upward dynamic is precisely what bears are watching closely. Phase Three: The flywheel breaks after mNAV falls below 1x. This is the true breaking point. Strategy buys BTC and deleverages by issuing MSTR common stock (mNAV > 1x) at a price higher than NAV. If BTC falls deep enough and mNAV falls below 1x, issuing common stock will dilute existing shareholder value, and Saylor will be unable to deleverage through the issuance. At that point, Strategy faces a trilemma: (a) continue issuing STRC at a higher dividend yield and accept higher leverage; (b) unilaterally reduce the dividend (25bps per month) according to SEC filing rules, allowing the STRC price to fall; (c) sell BTC to enter a falling market. Saylor has repeatedly claimed that he will never sell BTC. BitMEX Research concludes that (b) is the most likely scenario: "Strategy will not sell Bitcoin; it will simply abandon the narrative of pursuing stability with STRC." All the pressure will be shifted to STRC holders. An early warning sign has been raised: During the week of April 6-12, 2026, MSTR's ATM mechanism issuance amounted to $0—all financing was completed through STRC ($1.00B, 10.028 million shares; MSTR 8-K). mNAV is so tight that Saylor is unwilling to risk diluting common stock. The preconditions for Phase Three have been partially triggered—the flywheel is already on one leg.

Quantitative Crash Scenario

Why is this different from UST/Terra:UST relies on an algorithmic minting mechanism, with its sole support being its endogenous token (LUNA). STRC is backed by real BTC, and Strategy has the discretion to choose to reduce dividends instead of being forcibly liquidated. STRC's floor is not zero—but rather a priority claim on remaining assets in bankruptcy liquidation. However, if BTC falls by more than 60% and remains low, this lower limit could be far below $100. The key variable is time. Previously, every STRC pullback was recovered within weeks because BTC rebounded. A true crash requires a sustained bear market (below $50K for more than 3 months), allowing the dividend increase mechanism to operate long enough to erode confidence. The longer the STRC remains below par with continuously increasing dividends, the more it resembles a company rolling over increasingly fragile debt at ever-increasing interest rates—a pattern with a very clear ending in the credit market. Capital Structure Priority: The liquidation order is: Convertible Bonds (approximately $8.2 billion) → STRF → STRC → STRK → STRD → MSTR Common Stock. STRC follows the $8.2 billion unsecured debt and STRF preferred stock. Industry Perspectives: "STRC risks are significantly higher than short-duration US Treasuries… Investors may feel somewhat offended when the music stops." — BitMEX Research, *A Bit of a Stretch* (November 2025) "The appropriate way to assess STRC risk is to look at it from the perspective of governance and hierarchy, rather than just focusing on payment risk." — Greg Cipolaro, Global Head of Research at NYDIG (March 2026) "It's similar to shorting a put option on Bitcoin asset coverage—profiting from the downside risk of BTC falling and eroding the asset's buffer." — NYDIG Research Report (March 2026)

The core disagreement among analysts lies here: The bulls believe STRC is currently the safest way to achieve an 11.5% return in the market; the bears believe it represents a mispriced credit risk disguised as a money market product. The bears' core concern directly corresponds to the dividend adjustment mechanism described above: STRC will not suddenly default, but will gradually repric—the longer BTC remains depressed, the more it will slide from a quasi-monetary instrument into a distressed yield product. This gradual decline is the real risk, not a sudden overnight collapse.

Inferences and Predictions

Bottom Line:STRC is a truly novel financial instrument that works beautifully in the environment it was designed for—BTC is steadily rising, capital markets are open, and mNAV>1x. Under these conditions, it offers an attractive 11.5% return with manageable volatility. However, its downside structure is asymmetrical: it earns coupon payments in good times, but bears concentrated, single-origin BTC credit risk in bad times. It's not a substitute for government bonds or diversified high-yield bonds, but rather a leveraged position betting on the continued operation of the Strategy BTC accumulation flywheel—just packaged as fixed income. Three New Signals (as of April 2026) Signal 1: First pause in dividend increases in April (as of April 1, 2026, CoinDesk). After seven consecutive increases (from 9% to 11.5%) between August 2025 and March 2026, Saylor kept the dividend yield unchanged in April. Two interpretations: (a) Demand stabilized at this yield level, bullish; (b) Strategy hit the yield-sensitive ceiling for traditional fixed-income buyers, bearish. This is the single most noteworthy signal to watch in May-June, and also the inflection point around which the mNAV trigger framework described above revolves. Signal Two: During the week of April 6-12, MSTR ATM raised $0, with all financing completed by STRC ($1.00B; MSTR 8-K, April 2026). At the current BTC price level, mNAV is so tight that Saylor is unwilling to risk diluting common stock by continuing to issue MSTR. The preconditions for the third stage of the death spiral have been partially triggered—the flywheel is spinning on one leg. Signal Three: Last week's average BTC purchase price was $71,902/coin, lower than Strategy's historical cost of $75,577/coin (as of April 12, 2026, MSTR 8-K). Strategy is buying into a weak market DCA. The flywheel is still spinning, but every marginal purchase is thinning the asset buffer, not thickening it—the exact opposite of the dynamics of the accumulation phase in 2024-2025. Investment Advice: Hold, wait for a better entry point and BTC to rise. Current Status: Hold existing positions; do not add to positions without a better signal. MSTR's mNAV has compressed to around 1.0x. STRC is still holding at $100 par value and paying an 11.5% dividend, reflecting that the dividend mechanism is still working as designed. However, the safety margin is very narrow. Re-entering conditions: BTC rises above $70-75K, and MSTR mNAV confirms above 1.1x for two consecutive weeks. At that time, STRC returns to around $100 and re-enters the conditional buy zone. Historically, the combination of buying below $95 followed by a BTC rebound has contributed 7-11% capital gains plus accumulated coupon income—but this only occurs in environments where BTC can rebound within weeks (August 2025, November 2025, February 2026). Whether the current pullback is a continuation of this pattern or a sign of a more prolonged bear market is a real unknown. Exit Signal: A sell assessment will be initiated when any of the following conditions occur: (a) MSTR mNAV falls below 1.0 times and remains so for more than two weeks; (b) STRC VWAP remains below $95 for four consecutive weeks; (c) BTC falls below $55K with significant volume.

data.title: The U.S. military has for the first time admitted to running a node on the Bitcoin network.
data.description:
2026YearAprilMonth22On April 22, 2026, at a hearing of the U.S. House Armed Services Committee, Admiral Samuel John Paparo Jr., Commander of the Indo-Pacific Command When asked about the national security value of digital assets, they gave an answer that surprised most people. "Currently, we are in the experimental phase. We are running a live node on the Bitcoin network. We are not mining Bitcoin. We use it for monitoring and are conducting multiple operational tests to leverage the Bitcoin protocol to protect and harden the network." This is the first time a U.S. active-duty combatant command has publicly acknowledged that it has directly connected to the Bitcoin peer-to-peer network. Key follow-up questions at the hearing. The day before the hearing, on April 21st, at the Senate Armed Services Committee hearing... Paparo stated that Bitcoin demonstrates enormous potential as a computer science tool, and through its proof-of-work protocol, it effectively imposes a higher cost on attackers than simple algorithmic security, essentially being a peer-to-peer, trustless value transfer network. At the time, he did not disclose any specific actions. The following day, at the hearing on April 22, Texas Republican Representative Lance Gooden raised questions by comparing Bitcoin holdings in China and the US. Should the US maintain its leading position in the Bitcoin space as it has with gold and oil? Paparo then revealed details about running nodes. Intriguingly, Paparo made no mention of reserve assets or prices during the two-day hearings. His framework is entirely based on cryptography, blockchain, and proof-of-work—a pure computer science language, not financial language. [Image of a Bitcoin node] A Bitcoin node is a computer that runs Bitcoin software. It synchronizes complete blockchain data and independently verifies every transaction and every block on the network. It does not produce Bitcoin or process user funds; it simply checks and determines data accuracy, transmission, and acceptance according to protocol rules. Currently, there are approximately 15,000 to 20,000 publicly accessible full nodes globally, in addition to an undisclosed number of nodes operating behind firewalls. The Indo-Pacific Command's node is one of these, neither special nor possessing any additional privileges within the network.

2026

PaparoSupported the GENIUS Act at the hearing, and said he supports anything that can maintain the dollar's global dominance. The military's technological experiments were thus framed within the overall direction of the US financial strategy, but in practice, different departments continued to operate independently. In China, however, the picture is quite different. Restrictions on trading and mining have been in place for years, and the concurrently developed consortium blockchain technology and digital yuan system prioritize the development of controllable blockchain applications, excluding open-source crypto assets. This divergence in the two countries' approaches to digital infrastructure formed the basis for questions raised at this military committee hearing: Should a leading position in the Bitcoin space be maintained, similar to how it is with gold and oil? And just as Paparo testified before Congress, in April 2026, during the US-Iran ceasefire, Iran began charging tolls on fully loaded oil tankers passing through the Strait of Hormuz, at a rate of approximately [amount missing] per barrel of oil. $1, requiring payment in cryptocurrency; Bitcoin is one of the payment options listed by the official website. Reports from blockchain analytics firms TRM Labs and Chainalysis show that since mid-March, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has been collecting tanker fees through intermediaries, using methods including RMB, Bitcoin, and stablecoins, with stablecoins actually handling the majority of the funds. Iran's cryptocurrency holdings are projected to reach approximately $7.8 billion by 2025. On April 24, the U.S. Treasury Department announced sanctions against several Iranian-linked cryptocurrency wallets, including Tether, and subsequently froze two of its Tron network addresses, totaling approximately [number missing]. 3.44 billion US dollars USDT. At this stage, BTC and USDT can be seen as an extension of the dollar's hegemony. This is a completely different use of the technology compared to the nodes operated by the US military. The US military is conducting technical observation and protocol testing, while Iran is using Bitcoin and stablecoins to bypass SWIFT and the US dollar settlement system, exchanging the right of passage through the Strait of Hormuz for on-chain revenue. One is entering the network, and the other is completing transactions through the network; two things happening almost simultaneously, but serving completely opposite purposes. The Paparo hearing, still evolving, saw a predominantly positive reaction from the crypto community. Bitcoin experienced a slight short-term rise that day, briefly surpassing $7.7w, but this did not trigger a frenzy. This indicates that market sentiment is maturing, gradually accepting this type of technology as the norm. However, the amount of information available is currently very limited. Details such as the number of nodes running, the specific tests being conducted, and whether on-chain analytics tools have been integrated are not publicly disclosed. Paparo has repeatedly emphasized the experimental phase, and discussions regarding digital assets in the 2027 National Defense Authorization Act, spearheaded by Congressman Gooden, are currently stalled in Congress. The US military has consistently followed a similar path in its approach to the internet, GPS, and the Tor network: first military testing, then civilian applications. Bitcoin appears to be taking a similar early approach. The military is initially treating it as serious computer science infrastructure; whether it will profoundly impact broader cyber warfare strategies remains to be seen. Looking at the bigger picture, Bitcoin has appeared in increasingly more contexts that were previously unlikely to emerge over the past two years. ETFs, discussions on national strategic reserves, stablecoin legislation, military committee hearings, and transit fees in the Strait of Hormuz. Different countries and institutions, based on their respective needs, are engaging with the same set of agreements in their own ways.

data.title: How to view Bitcoin on-chain assets
data.description: At the end of the article on April 23rd, a reader left a long comment discussing various aspects of Bitcoin, including on-chain assets, DMT-NAT, and decentralization. I largely agree with their views on Bitcoin, Ethereum, and decentralization. This article mainly shares my views on DMT-NAT and other Bitcoin on-chain assets. Regarding DMT-NAT, this Bitcoin on-chain asset, I bought some when it first emerged, and I shared my views on this asset in an article from 2013 to 2014 (I think it was a Q&A section). However, I can't find the specific article now. Like other once-popular Bitcoin on-chain assets (such as inscriptions, runes, BRC-20, SRC-20, etc.), they are still in my possession. Every time I see them, they remind me of my past hopes for Bitcoin on-chain assets and the Bitcoin ecosystem. Each time I recall and summarize that period of history, I always reflect on some of the experiences and lessons learned. I will try my best to share and summarize my views on these Bitcoin on-chain assets in relatively simple language. Whether it's DMT-NAT or any on-chain Bitcoin asset, we can generally view them as being implemented through two steps: The first step is to write the information representing the characteristics and state of these assets into the Bitcoin chain in character form. The second step is to use an algorithm that can recognize this information to interpret what it represents. Which part of this information represents the asset type, is it DMT-NAT or BRC-20? Which part represents the asset quantity, is it 100 or...? 1000; which part represents the asset holder, is it account A or account B? ...... In these two steps, the first step has a smaller problem and can be considered close to decentralization. To some extent, this information, once written to the Bitcoin chain, is not easily tampered with. The second step, however, is the key to the entire implementation process and also its weakness. Anyone who has participated in these Bitcoin on-chain assets should remember that when participating in an asset (such as DMT-NAT), you are always told to download a XXX wallet. Why is it specifically required to tell participants to download a XXX wallet instead of simply asking them to download any Bitcoin wallet or providing a broad list of wallets? This is where the second step above comes into play. As I just mentioned, to interpret the state of this asset (i.e., to interpret the information written to the Bitcoin chain), there must be an algorithm capable of recognizing this information. However, this algorithm is not part of the general Bitcoin consensus protocol; it is only a specific protocol, or rather, a small-scale consensus formed within a small circle (such as the DMT-NAT community and enthusiasts). Therefore, to be able to parse and support this type of asset (DMT-NAT), a specific algorithm needs to be written into the wallet, and only a few cutting-edge wallet developers, not all Bitcoin wallet developers, can do this. Specifically regarding the asset DMT-NAT, wallets that can support it must be those that support specific protocols such as Ordinals and Tap Protocol, rather than general Bitcoin wallets. Let's take the implementation process of DMT-NAT as an example. The specific process of the first step above is: using inscription technology (inscription with the Ordinal protocol) to write its asset information onto the Bitcoin blockchain. The specific process of the second step above is as follows: Use a wallet capable of parsing the Digital Matter Theory (DMT) protocol to interpret the asset information read from the Bitcoin blockchain. More specifically, the DMT protocol requires wallets to parse the following fields (see the link at the end of the article for more details): "p": "tap", "op": "dmt-deploy", "tick": "nat", The definitions of the above three fields are... This is unique to DMT-NAT. Other Bitcoin on-chain assets, such as BRC-20, have different definitions for the above three fields, as follows: "p": "brc-20", "op": "mint", "tick": "ordi", By comparison, we can see that each Bitcoin on-chain asset has its own unique definition, and therefore its own unique parsing algorithm. However, these parsing algorithms are not part of the Bitcoin universal consensus protocol. Therefore, currently, only a very small number of pioneering nodes among the entire Bitcoin network nodes support this protocol. These nodes are willing to contribute to promoting this ecosystem, or are willing to lead this ecosystem, or for various other reasons. Because the support for these assets is limited to only a very small number of nodes/wallets, the risk of centralization becomes apparent. If, in extreme circumstances, these few nodes are all wiped out (technically, this isn't difficult to achieve; it just depends on whether the attacker has any benefit or whether the cost-benefit ratio is worthwhile), then the entire DMT-NAT asset will be in grave danger. This is why I say this is the key and the weakness in the whole process. DMT-NAT is the same for any other Bitcoin on-chain asset (including inscriptions, etc.). Of course, if we visit the relevant websites for DMT-NAT today, we'll find that there are "quite a few" wallets that support it. These include not only Bitcoin wallets but also Ethereum wallets (such as LittleFox), and even Uniswap supports it for transactions. How is this possible? How can Ethereum also support Bitcoin assets? In reality, the so-called "DMT-NAT" assets supported by Ethereum wallets and exchanges are not native to the Bitcoin network, but rather "packaged assets" that have been transferred from Bitcoin to Ethereum by enthusiasts or small teams using cross-chain bridges. Fundamentally, these assets, when traced back to their origins, still come down to those specific nodes that support DMT-NAT/wallets. Returning to the previously mentioned centralization risk, how do we address this risk? In my view, there are only two ways: One is to form a new blockchain network with strong consensus, where all nodes support this resolution algorithm. This network can then be used to thoroughly resolve and support DMT-NAT assets. The second approach is to completely integrate this specific algorithm/specific protocol (i.e., the DMT-NAT protocol) into Bitcoin's general consensus protocol. The first approach is quite difficult. How can the initiators of DMT-NAT get a vast number of node developers and wallet developers across the Bitcoin ecosystem to participate in building this network and supporting the protocol in their products? Should it be based on incentives or a grand vision? In fact, even the most consensus-driven inscription asset in the entire Bitcoin ecosystem (BRC-20) has not achieved this yet. The second method is the most thorough and permanent solution. As long as the team controlling the Bitcoin universal protocol (CORE) approves adding the DMT protocol to the next Bitcoin update, everything will be fine. But the question is, would the CORE team, which controls the general Bitcoin protocol, be willing? The answer is obvious. They even strongly rejected the inscription protocol, so why would they be willing to accept DMT-NAT? Don't mention the CORE team. I believe many readers of this article are thinking: Bitcoin doesn't need expansion, it doesn't need any on-chain assets, just pure Bitcoin is fine. Some might say, then why not just have a hard fork, letting the enthusiasts and community who support and promote these assets create a "new Bitcoin" that fully supports these Bitcoin on-chain assets? Theoretically and technically, there's nothing wrong with doing this, but the question is, after the fork, will the broader industry consensus recognize the "old Bitcoin" or this "new Bitcoin"? I believe it will still recognize the "old Bitcoin." Therefore, from any perspective, the consensus on this on-chain asset within the entire Bitcoin ecosystem is actually not strong, and I estimate it will be difficult for it to become stronger in the future. I once had high hopes for Bitcoin's on-chain assets and the Bitcoin ecosystem. If the widespread and enthusiastic collective innovation movement within the Bitcoin ecosystem at that time could have been embraced by the CORE team, driving them to upgrade and expand the Bitcoin protocol, then Bitcoin could truly have caught up with or even surpassed Ethereum in terms of ecosystem. However, subsequent realities have forced me to admit that this path is highly unlikely to be viable, and even more difficult to follow in the future. Therefore, I think it's fine for Bitcoin assets and the Bitcoin ecosystem to be a niche hobby and exploration, but it will be extremely difficult for them to become a robust ecosystem and develop into an asset with strong consensus. However, as I've said in previous articles, and I'd like to reiterate: I continue to admire and support those enthusiasts who have been working and contributing to the Bitcoin ecosystem. If they succeed, all the better, but even if they don't, they might stumble upon some unexpected innovations, which is still remarkable. Many major inventions and innovations in the history of science and technology were not planned in advance; they were often discovered accidentally and unintentionally.

data.title: Super Thursday is here; the Federal Reserve stages "Powell's last dance."
data.description:
On April 29th (early morning to early morning of April 30th Beijing time), Alphabet (Google), Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft will release their quarterly results after the market closes on the same trading day. Simultaneously, the Federal Reserve's FOMC will conclude its two-day meeting and announce its interest rate decision, and Fed Chairman Powell will hold his final press conference of his term. The Bank of England and the European Central Bank will also announce their latest interest rate decisions on the same day.

Northwestern Mutual's chief portfolio manager, Matt Stucky, characterized this day as "one of the most important earnings days in recent years."

The four companies have a combined market capitalization of approximately $11.6 trillion, representing over 19% of the S&P 500 index. Fluctuations in the performance of any one of them are enough to impact the broader market. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq indices have rebounded by approximately 11% and 18% respectively from their recent lows, with funds flowing back into the technology and data center sectors. The core narrative of this rebound is the continued expansion of AI investment. This earnings report will be a key test of whether this narrative can continue.

Thursday's schedule overview, compiled by Wall Street Insights

A $650 billion AI bill, the market is looking for returns

Funds are flowing back into technology, data centers, and related infrastructure

Powell's "last dance," interest rate expectations are already priced in

On the same day, the Federal Reserve's FOMC will conclude its two-day meeting and announce its interest rate decision.

On the same day, the Federal Reserve's FOMC will conclude its two-day meeting and announce its interest rate decision.

The market has already fully priced in maintaining interest rates in the 3.50% to 3.75% range. Following the rate cuts scheduled for the second half of 2025, the Fed paused, and rising oil prices further complicated the inflation outlook, pushing back the window for further rate cuts. This will also be Powell's last press conference as Fed Chairman. His term expires on May 15th, and Trump's nominee, Warsh, is expected to be confirmed by the Senate before the Fed's next meeting in mid-June. The biggest question now is whether Powell will announce that he will remain a member of the Fed's Board of Governors after stepping down as chairman. Geopolitical Risks: Strait of Hormuz Impacts AI Supply Chain Geopolitical tensions added an extra variable to the day. The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz directly impacted global energy flows and supply chains, with data center supply chains also affected. Moody's analyst Terrence Dennehy specifically pointed out in a research report last week that the Middle East conflict poses a "supply risk" to the helium market. "Helium is crucial in multiple stages of semiconductor manufacturing—including cooling, carrier gas use, and leak detection—and there are no effective substitutes," Dennehy wrote. Northwestern Mutual's Stucky also stated that the possibility of the four companies further raising their capital expenditure forecasts cannot be ruled out, which could trigger a new round of market concerns about over-investment in AI. After the earnings reports, how will the market price them? This earnings report is more like a screening point than a master switch. Wall Street Insights mentioned that there are currently no signs of the AI ​​theme ending, and funds are still flowing in. However, the market's pricing logic is diverging: companies with faster delivery, stronger orders, and more stable profits will continue to command a premium; companies with significant investment but unclear return paths will experience more pronounced stock price volatility. The semiconductor, server, network equipment, and data center equipment industry chain still most directly follows the investments of major manufacturers. The recent divergence between software and chips indicates that the market is beginning to concentrate on sectors closer to orders and infrastructure within this main theme. If the four companies' financial reports continue to confirm the intensity of demand and capital expenditure, this divergence will only become more pronounced. On the day of the earnings reports, the market's most pressing questions are: will the full-year capital expenditure figure be revised upwards again? Can the growth rate of cloud business continue to rise? Will AI-related revenue be disclosed more clearly? And will there be more significant pressure on profit margins and cash flow?

data.title: The most crypto enthusiasts are becoming the least crypto enthusiasts.
data.description:
Hong Kong Carnival × Bangkok Money 20/20 Observation Notes

At Hong Kong Blockchain Week this April, what impressed me most wasn't any particular panel, but a scene.

Around 10 PM, in a tea restaurant in Wan Chai, four or five people were crammed around a table, eating stir-fried beef noodles and chatting about their next plans. A friend who used to work on stablecoin payments said their team had completely shifted to AI; another, who worked on on-chain data, said he now spent half his time helping AI companies build data pipelines.

No one was talking about coin prices, no one was talking about narratives, and even the term Web3 barely appeared.

My initial feeling wasn't surprise, but a strange sense of familiarity—these people, sitting at the same table three years ago, must have been talking about DeFi, NFTs, and blockchain games. They were still the same people, just as excited, just as fully engaged. This time, after a trip to the Hong Kong Carnival and Bangkok Money 20/20, one sentence kept replaying in my mind: The most crypto-savvy people are becoming the least crypto-savvy. After the Web3 tide recedes, what's left? After these two trips, I think I have my own answer. I. Hong Kong: Familiar Faces, Unfamiliar Topics Let's start with Hong Kong. This year's carnival saw a noticeable decrease in cryptocurrency projects; the bustling atmosphere of the past two years, with everyone handing out T-shirts and telling stories, was gone. This year's official theme was "Mountains, Wind, Clouds, and Sea," clearly positioning itself as a farewell to cryptocurrency hype. Three years ago, this statement would have drawn boos from the audience. But this year, no one felt anything was amiss, because everyone had already stopped talking about cryptocurrencies, creating a tacit understanding. Walking around the exhibition, the faces were familiar: OKX Wallet, TRON, ZA Bank, HashKey, and NewFire. But the topics they discussed changed, focusing heavily on two words: RWA and AI. RWA continued its momentum from last year, but frankly, everyone knows who's genuinely working on projects and who's just putting on a show. One assessment I believe holds true: for Hong Kong, RWA is essentially the productization of wealth management and investment—moving real-world assets onto the blockchain for more efficient and easier cross-border distribution. This is precisely what Hong Kong excels at: institutional design and financial productization. With the bubble bursting, Hong Kong is actually more comfortable—the restlessness that never truly belonged to it has finally dissipated. The AI ​​section was more interesting. Almost every panel discussed the combination of AI and Web3, but after attending several sessions, frankly, most discussions remained at the level of "these two things should be combined." As for how to combine them or what problems they solve, no one could explain it clearly. My feeling is that Web3 is being paired with AI not because they've figured it out, but because if they don't combine them, there's really no story to tell. And the speakers on stage probably knew they were just putting it together. But survival comes first; that's the survival philosophy of this industry. There's been little new news about Hong Kong dollar stablecoins. Licenses have been issued, but after asking around, the two major banks are each taking their own pace, not rushing to make a big splash, and it seems nobody cares. But what truly impressed me were the people in the audience. The busiest people in the venue weren't the guests, but those casually dressed, wearing exhibition badges, moving back and forth in the negotiation area—business development, community building, content creation, and connecting projects with resources. They didn't have impressive resumes, and their speech wasn't necessarily "professional," but their understanding of the industry grew from countless meals and setbacks. This understanding wasn't gained from reading reports; it was earned over time. Whether an industry can weather economic cycles depends not only on the number of star companies at the top, but also on how many people are willing to persevere even without applause. The foundation of Web3 remains. But what runs on top of that foundation has completely changed. II. Bangkok: The Trojan Horse of Stablecoins Flying from Hong Kong to Bangkok, the atmosphere shifts dramatically. Money 20/20 is a purely fintech B2B expo; the entrance fee is not cheap, and attendees are dressed as if they are going to meet clients. The panel area often had empty seats, but the adjacent business meeting area was packed from opening to closing. Surprisingly, stablecoin and crypto-native companies made up about a third of the exhibitors. OSL, Circle, Ripple, Fireblocks, Cobo, Pyth… at least a dozen companies, many of them first-time exhibitors. Money 20/20 even added a dedicated Intersection zone this year, positioned as the intersection of TradFi and DeFi—stablecoins are no longer on the fringes of the fintech expo; they're part of the main agenda. Interestingly, none of these third of crypto companies were selling crypto on their booths. They were selling payment chains, settlement channels, and asset custody. Some exhibitors simply defined themselves as "Web 2.5 finance"—one foot in native crypto and the other in traditional payments. Those discussing business didn't care what blockchain was running underneath; they only wanted three things: fast transfers, low costs, and compliance. I sat in the negotiation area for two afternoons, and every ten minutes at the next table, I heard someone mention stablecoin. No one talked about the price; everyone was talking about how to build the network, how to connect with merchants, and which solution to use for compliance. Everyone there had businesses to implement. At one panel, the host directly challenged the guests: "Pix in Brazil already offers instant, free transfers, why are you even working on stablecoins?" The answer was straightforward—Pix solves domestic problems, but it's still ineffective for cross-border payments. This is probably the most honest positioning of stablecoin payments: it doesn't replace local payment systems, but fills the gap in cross-border payments—something traditional finance has never done well. Thanks to Finternet for the invitation, I had an interview with Sumsub, and it left a very deep impression on me. This company, which provides KYC/KYB services, initially had only Web3 projects as clients—exchanges, wallets, and DeFi protocols. But now, their biggest incremental clients are actually from Web2: payment institutions, banks, and companies expanding overseas. Their massive Web3 client base has become a strong endorsement, allowing them to more smoothly penetrate the traditional financial market. Web3 gave them experience; Web2 is the real market. See, this is the footnote to what I said: The most crypto-savvy people are becoming the least crypto-savvy. Stablecoins are no longer just "entering" traditional finance; they've completely integrated—to the point that at trade shows, you can't distinguish between stablecoin companies and fintech firms. Even if traditional financial institutions don't do stablecoin business themselves, their clients will force them to adopt it. Stablecoins didn't storm the castle of traditional finance through the front door. They entered through the back door, and by the time those inside discovered it, the passage was already paved. III. AI Label Inflation The channels are laid out, but new labels have been affixed to them. At the Bangkok exhibition, I counted, and about eight out of ten booths I passed had "AI" or "Agentic" printed on them—Agentic Payment, Agentic Wallets, Agentic Banking. I asked a few companies in detail: What is your AI module's most mature use case? The answers were rather vague, basically pointing to the future scenario of A2A (Agent-to-Agent). As for actual transaction volume, everyone tacitly refrained from providing figures. A company that focused on stablecoin payments a few years ago made a choice many had considered but hadn't yet implemented. With the infrastructure layer already congested, building more channels would mean squeezing into a sea of ​​similar ones. Instead of waiting for the water to come, they chose a different approach, venturing into payment solutions for the booming AI industry. They weren't just labeling AI, but providing services for it. Compared to the vague A2A concepts at the exhibition, this was a much clearer approach: don't wait for agents to pay themselves; address the payment pain points that AI companies already face. However, returning to the AI ​​hype at the exhibition, this scene does resemble Web3 in 2021—infrastructure first, killer applications still unknown. However, there's a difference: in 2021, demand was created out of thin air to find users, while today's agentic payment has at least one real premise—AI agents are indeed growing exponentially, and sooner or later they will need to pay and collect payments themselves. The question isn't whether the demand exists, but when and in what form it will arrive. During the window of "when it will arrive," labeling is the safest option. What if it does arrive? IV. After the channels are laid, then what? Looking at Hong Kong and Bangkok together, a clear distinction emerges. Hong Kong focuses on financial productization—RWA, wealth management, asset management—competing on product design and distribution channels, combined with the operational strategies of the cryptocurrency world. Bangkok focuses on payment channels—cross-border settlement of stablecoins—competing on compliance licenses and local channels. These two paths combined represent what blockchain truly leaves behind after the Web3 hype—financial infrastructure. It's not the DeFi Summer's yield frenzy, nor the NFT frenzy of FOMO. It's about channels, licenses, and partners. It's boring, but real. Web3's initial promise was "decentralized reconstruction of everything." What survives after the hype fades are patches and extensions of the centralized financial system. The cypherpunk revolution didn't happen. But the pipeline was laid inside the walls—this in itself may last longer than the revolution. The pipeline is laid, but three questions remain unresolved: Is it too late to build stablecoin infrastructure? There are already too many companies doing infrastructure at the Bangkok trade show, and the space for differentiation is rapidly narrowing. New entrants don't need to build more pipelines, but rather to find out what kind of water should flow through them—whoever can embed stablecoins into high-frequency, essential scenarios will be the winner of the next stage. It's not about who builds the pipelines, it's about who uses them. Application solutions are the direction. With a robust infrastructure layer, value is beginning to migrate to the application layer. Companies that laid out broadband in the 2000s made the first wave of profits; the real big business came from Taobao and WeChat, which later built upon it. Stablecoins are approaching that inflection point. What about Agentic Payments? I've been tracking this area for a while now. Visa, Mastercard, and Stripe are all making moves, and the x402 protocol is also progressing. But the gap between protocol and implementation isn't technology; it's a trust framework and a sufficiently large cross-border transaction scenario. Otherwise, it will remain just demos and panels. However, back in 2021, when someone first talked about stablecoin cross-border payments, they probably received the same treatment—"The concept makes sense, but implementation is still a long way off." Five years later, stablecoins have already integrated into the capillaries of traditional finance. Agentic payments may be at the same stage. It's just that this window of opportunity will be much shorter. On the return flight, what kept replaying in my mind wasn't the content of those panels, but the table in the tea restaurant. One person switched to AI, another is helping an AI company build data pipelines, and the rest are still discussing how to integrate stablecoin payments into more merchants. Three years ago they were talking about a different world, but one thing hasn't changed—they're still there, still working, still throwing themselves into the pool. The most unique thing about the Web3 community isn't how cutting-edge the technology is, but its natural attraction for these kinds of people—who jump in regardless of how cold the water is. The tracks may change, the narratives may shift, but this raw sense of participation won't disappear. It's just changed its clothes. After the tide receded, no revolution occurred. But the most crypto-savvy people, with their tactics, speed, and survival instincts, are infiltrating larger battlefields like traditional finance, AI, and cross-border payments. They no longer shout slogans, but they're more dangerous than before. Because this time, they're wearing suits.
